title: React & TypeScript
description: Master React with TypeScript — component typing, hooks, generics, utility types, and real-world patterns for production-ready applications.

modules.title: TypeScript Fundamentals
modules.description: Type inference, interfaces vs type aliases, and generics.

sections.title: Type Inference & Reassignment

choices.C: Compile-time error — 'string' is not assignable to type 'number'

sections.explanation: TypeScript infers `score` as `number` from its initializer `100`. Attempting to assign a `string` is caught at compile time — not at runtime — making bugs impossible to ship.

sections.title: Interface vs Type — Declaration Merging
sections.body_markdown: Which statement is **true** about `interface` vs `type` in TypeScript?

choices.B: `interface` supports declaration merging; re-declaring the same `type` alias is an error
choices.C: `type` can be implemented by a class but `interface` cannot
choices.D: They are completely interchangeable with no differences

sections.explanation: Declaration merging lets you declare the same `interface` name multiple times and TypeScript merges the definitions. This is used by libraries to let consumers augment types. `type` aliases cannot be redeclared.

sections.title: Generics — Explicit Type Argument

choices.C: Compile-time error — Argument of type 'number' is not assignable to parameter of type 'string'

sections.explanation: We explicitly supply `T = string`, so TypeScript expects a `string` argument. Passing `42` (a `number`) is a compile-time type mismatch. Omitting `<string>` would let TypeScript infer `T = number` and compile fine.

modules.title: Advanced TypeScript Types
modules.description: Union types, type narrowing, keyof, and utility types.

sections.title: Union Type Narrowing

choices.D: Type error — cannot call toFixed on a union type

sections.explanation: Inside the `typeof val === 'number'` guard, TypeScript narrows `val` to `number`. `(3.14159).toFixed(2)` returns the string `"3.14"`. Outside the guard, `val` is narrowed to `string`.

sections.title: The keyof Operator

choices.A: Compiles fine — 'age' is a valid string
choices.B: Type error — 'age' is not assignable to type '"id" | "name" | "email"'

sections.explanation: `keyof User` produces the union literal `'id' | 'name' | 'email'`. Assigning `'age'` — not in that union — is a compile-time error. Use `keyof` with generics to write safe property-access helpers like `function get<T, K extends keyof T>(obj: T, key: K): T[K]`.

sections.title: Partial & Readonly Utility Types

choices.B: Type error — cannot assign to 'host' because it is a read-only property

sections.explanation: `Partial<Config>` makes all fields optional (so `{ host: 'localhost' }` is valid). `Readonly<...>` then makes all fields immutable. Any mutation attempt is a compile-time error. The two utility types compose independently.

modules.title: React Component Typing
modules.description: Typing props, events, and refs in React with TypeScript.

sections.title: React.FC<Props> — What Does It Give You?

choices.B: React.FC<Props> enforces prop types at compile time and types the JSX return value
choices.C: React.FC<Props> adds lifecycle methods to the function
choices.D: React.FC<Props> is required for hooks to work

sections.explanation: `React.FC<Props>` (alias for `React.FunctionComponent<Props>`) annotates the function so TypeScript checks every call-site's props against `Props`. It also types the return as `JSX.Element | null`. Note: modern React (v18+) removed the implicit `children` prop from FC.

sections.title: Typing onChange Events
sections.explanation: React wraps native browser events in `SyntheticEvent`. For `<input>` onChange the precise type is `React.ChangeEvent<HTMLInputElement>`, which exposes `e.target.value` as `string` and `e.target.checked` as `boolean`. The generic parameter is the DOM element type.

sections.title: useRef with DOM Elements — Null Safety

choices.A: useRef doesn't work with DOM elements — use createRef instead
choices.B: `current` could be null before the element mounts — needs a null check
choices.C: No issue — TypeScript knows current is always set after mount

sections.explanation: `useRef<HTMLInputElement>(null)` initializes `current` as `null` and TypeScript types it as `HTMLInputElement | null`. Calling `.focus()` directly errors: 'Object is possibly null'. The fix: `if (inputRef.current) inputRef.current.focus()`.

modules.title: React Hooks & TypeScript
modules.description: useState, useEffect, useMemo, useCallback — typed correctly.

sections.title: useState — The never[] Pitfall

choices.A: Works fine — TypeScript infers number[] from the setter call
choices.B: Type error — TypeScript infers `never[]` for `[]`; numbers are not assignable to never
choices.C: Works fine — empty array is typed as any[]
choices.D: Runtime error — useState doesn't accept arrays

sections.explanation: TypeScript infers `useState([])` as `never[]` because it cannot determine the element type from an empty literal. Numbers are not assignable to `never`. Fix: `useState<number[]>([])` to provide the generic type explicitly.

sections.title: useEffect Cleanup Function

choices.A: The return function re-runs the effect on every render
choices.B: The return function is the cleanup — runs on unmount (or before the next effect run when deps change)
choices.C: Returning a function from useEffect is a type error in TypeScript
choices.D: The cleanup only runs when a dependency array value changes

sections.explanation: useEffect's return value must be `void` or a cleanup function `() => void`. With `[]` deps it runs only on unmount. This clears the interval and prevents memory leaks. Without cleanup, the interval would keep firing after the component is removed from the DOM.

sections.title: useMemo vs useCallback
sections.body_markdown: What is the **key difference** between `useMemo` and `useCallback`?

choices.A: useMemo is for async operations; useCallback is for sync operations
choices.B: useMemo memoizes a computed **value**; useCallback memoizes a **function reference**
choices.C: useCallback re-computes on every render; useMemo never re-computes
choices.D: They are identical — useCallback is just an alias of useMemo

sections.explanation: `useMemo(() => expensiveCalc(a, b), [a, b])` caches the **result**. `useCallback((x) => handler(a, x), [a])` caches the **function reference** — equivalent to `useMemo(() => (x) => handler(a, x), [a])`. Use useCallback to stabilize callbacks passed to memoized children.

modules.title: Patterns & Performance
modules.description: React.memo, typed Context, and discriminated union props.

sections.title: React.memo — Shallow Comparison

choices.A: Child re-renders every time the parent renders
choices.B: Child only re-renders when the `value` prop changes (shallow equality check)
choices.C: Child never re-renders after the first mount
choices.D: Child re-renders only when its own internal state changes

sections.explanation: `React.memo` wraps a component and performs a **shallow comparison** of previous vs next props. If `value` is still `42`, the render is skipped. Caveat: for object/array/function props, reference equality is checked — a new object literal `{}` is always a different reference, breaking memoization.

sections.title: Typed Context with Null Guard

choices.A: The throw is required by React's rules for custom hooks
choices.B: It narrows the type from `ThemeCtx | null` to `ThemeCtx` AND provides a clear developer error when used outside the provider
choices.C: It prevents the context from triggering re-renders
choices.D: It is unnecessary — useContext never returns null

sections.explanation: TypeScript knows `ctx` is `ThemeCtx | null`. After the `if (!ctx) throw`, control-flow analysis narrows the type to `ThemeCtx` for the rest of the function — no assertion needed. The throw also produces a clear error message instead of a confusing 'Cannot read properties of null'.

sections.title: Discriminated Union Props

choices.A: This causes runtime errors because TypeScript types are erased
choices.B: TypeScript narrows the union via `variant`, making each branch's exclusive props available type-safely
choices.C: Both `href` and `onClick` are available in both branches
choices.D: This requires a type assertion (`as`) to access href

sections.explanation: A **discriminated union** uses a shared literal property (`variant`) as a discriminant. Inside `variant === 'link'`, TypeScript narrows `props` to `{ variant: 'link'; href: string }` — accessing `href` is safe and `onClick` doesn't exist. This pattern eliminates invalid prop combinations at compile time.
